name: Priya Sharma
cv:
PRIYA SHARMA
priya.sharma@email.com | LinkedIn: linkedin.com/in/priyasharma-de | London, UK

SENIOR DATA ENGINEER

PROFESSIONAL SUMMARY
Data engineer with 6 years of experience building production data pipelines in regulated financial environments. Specialist in Python, SQL, dbt, and Snowflake. Proven track record delivering low-latency data products for trading and payments teams.

EXPERIENCE

Senior Data Engineer — Barclays Corporate & Investment Bank, London (2021–present)
- Designed and owned the trade reconciliation pipeline processing 500k+ events/day using Python and Kafka
- Migrated 40+ legacy ETL jobs to dbt + Airflow, reducing maintenance overhead by 60%
- Introduced Great Expectations for data quality validation across the data warehouse (Snowflake)
- Mentored 2 junior engineers; led sprint planning for a team of 5

Data Engineer — GoCardless, London (2019–2021)
- Built and maintained real-time payment reconciliation pipelines using Python and BigQuery
- Developed internal dbt models for financial reporting used by the CFO function
- Collaborated with data science team to productionise fraud detection features

Junior Data Engineer — Accenture, London (2018–2019)
- Delivered SQL-based ETL pipelines for a retail banking client migration project

SKILLS
Python, SQL, dbt, Snowflake, BigQuery, Apache Airflow, Apache Kafka, Great Expectations, Spark (basic), Git, Docker

EDUCATION
BSc Computer Science — University of Edinburgh (2015–2018)

RIGHT TO WORK
British citizen
linkedin: I'm a data engineer with six years in fintech and payments, specialising in building reliable data infrastructure that finance teams can actually trust. Currently at Barclays CIB, where I lead pipeline engineering for the trade operations data platform. I've spent most of my career in Python and SQL, with a strong focus on dbt and Snowflake. Before Barclays I was at GoCardless helping scale their data platform during a period of rapid growth. I care deeply about data quality — if the numbers aren't right, nothing downstream matters. I'm based in London and open to senior data engineering roles, ideally in a fast-paced financial or fintech environment.
interview_transcript:
Interviewer: Thanks for coming in, Priya. Can you start by telling me a bit about your current role?

Priya: Sure. I'm a senior data engineer at Barclays CIB. My main focus is the trade reconciliation platform — we process around half a million events a day and latency is critical. I own the pipeline architecture end-to-end, from Kafka ingestion through to Snowflake and the dbt models that feed the reconciliation dashboards.

Interviewer: What draws you to this role specifically?

Priya: A few things. I've been at Barclays for nearly four years now and I'm ready for a new challenge in a smaller, faster-moving environment. Apex's payments infrastructure focus is really aligned with what I've been doing. I want to stay in fintech.

Interviewer: How do you feel about the onsite requirement? We're five days a week in Canary Wharf.

Priya: That works perfectly for me. I actually prefer being in the office — I find I collaborate better in person, and for a role like this where you're working closely with quant analysts, being present matters. I live in Bethnal Green so the commute is easy.

Interviewer: Great. And salary expectations?

Priya: I'm currently on £78,000 and I'm looking for something in the £82–87,000 range depending on the full package.

Interviewer: Do you have any questions for us?

Priya: Yes — what does the on-call rotation look like for the data platform team?

name: Tomás Novák
cv:
TOMÁS NOVÁK
tomas.novak@email.com | Prague → London, UK

DATA ENGINEER

PROFESSIONAL SUMMARY
Data engineer with 5 years of experience in financial data infrastructure. Expert in Python, dbt, and cloud data warehouses. EU citizen with indefinite leave to remain in the UK. Available immediately.

EXPERIENCE

Data Engineer — Revolut, London (2020–2024)
- Built and maintained dbt models powering Revolut's internal financial reporting and regulatory compliance pipelines
- Led migration from legacy SQL scripts to dbt, creating 80+ tested models in Snowflake
- Built Airflow DAGs for daily reconciliation and month-end close processes
- Worked closely with the compliance and finance teams to ensure data accuracy for FCA reporting

Data Analyst → Data Engineer — Monzo Bank, London (2019–2020)
- Transitioned from analytics to engineering role; built first automated pipeline for chargeback data
- Maintained BigQuery data models for product analytics

SKILLS
Python (pandas, pyspark), SQL, dbt, Snowflake, BigQuery, Airflow, Kafka (consumer), Terraform, Docker, GitHub Actions

EDUCATION
BSc Mathematics — Charles University, Prague (2016–2019)

LANGUAGES
English (fluent), Czech (native), Slovak (fluent)

RIGHT TO WORK
EU citizen, UK ILR holder — no sponsorship required
linkedin: Data engineer focused on financial data infrastructure and regulatory reporting. Five years in London fintech — Revolut and Monzo — building the plumbing that keeps compliance and finance teams happy. I specialise in dbt and Snowflake and have a soft spot for well-tested data models. Czech national with UK ILR, so no visa complications. Looking for a senior data engineering role in a regulated financial environment where data quality genuinely matters. Happy to be in the office — I find async-only remote cultures don't work well for data engineering.
interview_transcript:
Interviewer: Thanks for coming in, Tomás. Tell me about your experience with dbt at Revolut.

Tomás: At Revolut I was effectively the dbt lead for the finance data platform. We had over 80 models, all tested with dbt tests and documented. I introduced the staging/intermediate/mart layering pattern there and trained the rest of the team on it. We were on Snowflake the whole time.

Interviewer: And how do you feel about Kafka? We use it heavily for payment events.

Tomás: I've worked as a consumer — reading from Kafka topics into our pipelines — but I haven't architected Kafka infrastructure from scratch. I'm confident I can get up to speed quickly on the producer side, but I want to be honest about the depth of my experience there.

Interviewer: That's fair. What are your thoughts on the office setup? Five days at Canary Wharf.

Tomás: No issue at all. I live in Wapping, so it's a ten-minute walk. Honestly I've been mostly in the office at Revolut too — I prefer it for engineering roles.

Interviewer: And your right to work situation?

Tomás: I have EU settled status with ILR, so I have the right to work indefinitely in the UK. No sponsorship needed.

Interviewer: Salary?

Tomás: I'm at £76,000 currently. I'd be looking for £80–85,000 for this move.

Interviewer: Perfect. We'll be in touch shortly.

cv:
AMARA OSEI

SENIOR PRODUCT MANAGER

Strategic product manager with 7 years of experience leading B2B SaaS products from 0 to 1 and through scaling phases. Deep expertise in workflow automation and enterprise procurement software. Experienced people manager with a track record of building high-performing PM teams.

EXPERIENCE

- Own the supplier management product line with 3 direct PM reports
- Drove 35% increase in supplier onboarding completion rate through a redesigned onboarding flow
- Partnered with enterprise sales to scope and deliver custom integrations for Fortune 500 customers
- Defined and shipped OKR-aligned roadmaps across 4 engineering squads

Product Manager — Procurify (Remote) (2019–2021)
- Led product for the approval workflow module; shipped 12 major features in 18 months
- Worked closely with customer success to reduce churn through targeted product improvements
- Introduced product analytics (Amplitude) and built a data-driven prioritisation framework

Associate PM — Zoho Corporation (Chennai, India) (2017–2019)
- Supported delivery of features for Zoho Books and Zoho Inventory

SKILLS
Product strategy, roadmapping, user research, SQL (intermediate), Amplitude, Mixpanel, Jira, Figma, A/B testing, OKRs, stakeholder management, people management

EDUCATION
MBA — INSEAD (2016–2017)
BSc Business Administration — University of Ghana (2012–2016)
linkedin: Product leader with seven years in B2B SaaS, specialising in procurement and workflow automation software. Currently at Coupa Software leading the supplier management product with a team of three PMs. I've spent my career building products that enterprise buyers actually adopt — which means obsessing over the onboarding experience and the moments where value becomes tangible. Remote-first advocate; I've worked fully distributed for four years across multiple time zones. I'm a strong believer in data-informed (not data-dictated) decisions. Open to senior PM or Head of Product roles at growth-stage B2B SaaS companies. Based in Accra, Ghana — UK or US visa sponsorship needed.
interview_transcript:
Interviewer: Welcome, Amara. Can you walk me through a product you're most proud of?

Amara: The supplier onboarding redesign at Coupa. When I joined, the onboarding completion rate was around 45% — suppliers were dropping off because the flow was too complex and the value wasn't clear early enough. I ran discovery with 20+ suppliers, identified the three biggest friction points, and shipped a redesigned flow over two quarters. Completion rate went to 80%. That project also strengthened my relationship with the enterprise sales team because it directly reduced their escalations.

Interviewer: How do you manage your PM team?

Amara: I have three direct reports at Coupa. I try to give them real ownership — they own their areas fully and I'm there to unblock, coach, and set context. I do weekly 1:1s and we do a monthly team retrospective.

Interviewer: What are your thoughts on the remote setup here? We're fully async-first.

Amara: Perfect for me. I've been fully remote for four years and I'm very comfortable with async communication. I'm in the GMT+0 zone, which works well for overlap with UK and European teams.

Interviewer: And visa? We do offer sponsorship.

Amara: Yes, I'd need a UK Global Talent or Skilled Worker visa. I've checked the requirements and I believe I qualify based on my experience. That's great that you offer sponsorship.

Interviewer: Salary expectations?

Amara: I'm currently earning the equivalent of £88,000. I'd be looking for £90–95,000 for this step up in seniority and location context.

linkedin: Product leader obsessed with building B2B SaaS products that sell themselves. I've spent eight years in workflow automation and spend management software — from Series A scrappiness to Series C scale. Currently leading product at Workato UK, where I have a team of four PMs and we're building the self-serve motion from scratch. I love PLG, data, and the puzzle of making enterprise software actually delightful. Remote-first but occasionally in London co-working spaces. Open to Head of Product or VP Product roles at growth-stage B2B companies.
interview_transcript:
Interviewer: Sarah-Jane, thanks for joining us. Tell me about your approach to PLG at Workato.

Sarah-Jane: At Workato we had a traditional sales-led model and I was brought in to build a PLG layer alongside it. The first thing I did was run activation experiments — we had a 14-day trial that most users weren't getting value from in time. We redesigned the first-run experience using data from Amplitude and cut time-to-first-automation from 4 days to 20 minutes. That unlocked a 40% lift in trial-to-paid conversion.

Interviewer: How do you manage your team?

Sarah-Jane: I have four direct reports — three senior PMs and one associate PM I've been mentoring. I focus on clarity of ownership, ambitious but achievable goals, and creating space for them to think strategically rather than just executing tickets. I do monthly strategy sessions with the team and quarterly offsites when we can.

Interviewer: This is a fully remote role. How do you feel about that?

Sarah-Jane: I've been remote since 2020 and it suits me very well. I have a proper home office setup and I'm very comfortable running async-first teams. I occasionally work from co-working spaces for the social element, but I don't need to be in an office.

Interviewer: Are you UK based with right to work?

Sarah-Jane: Yes, British citizen, no issues there.

Interviewer: And salary?

Sarah-Jane: I'm at £92,000 currently. I'd be looking for £95,000 for this role.

name: Dr. James Caldwell
cv:
DR. JAMES CALDWELL
james.caldwell@email.com | Cambridge, UK

ML ENGINEER

PROFESSIONAL SUMMARY
Applied ML engineer with 6 years of experience in computer vision and sensor fusion systems for defence and public safety applications. Active DV clearance holder. Expert in Python, PyTorch, and model deployment pipelines.

EXPERIENCE

ML Engineer — QinetiQ, Malvern/Cambridge (2020–present)
- Designed and trained convolutional and transformer-based perception models for airborne EO/IR sensors
- Led integration of ONNX-optimised models into embedded inference systems (NVIDIA Jetson)
- Developed MLflow experiment tracking framework adopted across 3 project teams
- Collaborated with signal processing team on radar-optical sensor fusion pipeline

Research Scientist (Computer Vision) — AWE, Aldermaston (2018–2020)
- Developed object detection models for nuclear facility surveillance systems
- Published one classified technical report and one open paper on domain adaptation

SKILLS
Python, PyTorch, ONNX, TensorRT, MLflow, OpenCV, NumPy, Git, Docker, Kubernetes (basic), C++ (basic), CUDA

EDUCATION
PhD Computer Vision — University of Edinburgh (2014–2018)
MEng Electronics — University of Cambridge (2010–2014)

CLEARANCE
Active DV clearance (UK government)

RIGHT TO WORK
British citizen
linkedin: Applied ML engineer with six years building perception systems for defence and public safety. I work at the intersection of deep learning and real-world deployment — training models that need to work reliably in the field, not just on benchmarks. Active DV clearance. Cambridge-based and comfortable with hybrid working arrangements. I care about hard problems with real stakes and prefer small, technically excellent teams. Open to senior ML engineer roles in defence, public safety, or aerospace.
interview_transcript:
Interviewer: James, thanks for coming in. Tell me about the perception work you've been doing at QinetiQ.

James: Sure. The core of my current role is developing and productionising perception models for airborne systems — mainly EO/IR cameras. We use PyTorch for training and then export to ONNX for deployment on embedded hardware. Latency and power constraints are real, so I've done a lot of model quantisation and TensorRT optimisation. Most recently I've been working on a sensor fusion system that combines radar and optical detections, which is quite an interesting problem.

Interviewer: And your clearance status?

James: I hold active DV clearance, which I've maintained since my time at AWE. It's current.

Interviewer: The role is 2 days in Cambridge, remote otherwise. How do you feel about that?

James: That works well. I live in Cambridge so the commute is easy. And I actually appreciate the flexibility — I tend to do my best deep work at home.

Interviewer: Are you interested in the compressed hours option we offer?

James: Possibly down the line, but it's not a requirement for me. Standard hours are fine.

Interviewer: Salary expectations?

James: I'm at £88,000 at QinetiQ. Given the DV clearance I'd be expecting £95–100,000.

Fatima: My main deployment work has been edge-focused — we take PyTorch models, export to ONNX, and deploy onto edge devices at transport hubs. I've done TensorRT optimisation a couple of times when latency was critical. I haven't done Kubernetes at scale but I'm comfortable with Docker.

Interviewer: And your clearance?

Fatima: I hold active SC clearance. I haven't been through DV, but I understand DV can be sponsored through your MoD contracts. I'm willing to undergo that process.

Interviewer: That's right, we can sponsor it. The 2-days-onsite model — does that work for you?

Fatima: Yes, I'm based in Cambridge so that's very convenient. I actually prefer having a couple of office days — I like the team environment.

Interviewer: Are you interested in compressed hours?

Fatima: A four-day week would actually be wonderful if it's possible. I'm doing some part-time postgraduate study and it would help with scheduling.

Interviewer: We can certainly explore that. Salary?

Fatima: I'm on £68,000 at the moment. I'd be looking for £75–80,000.

linkedin: Senior data engineer building data platforms for European fintech and banking clients. Five years of experience with Python, dbt, and Kafka. Currently working fully remote for Mediobanca from Milan. I've worked across risk data, payments reconciliation, and regulatory reporting — environments where accuracy is non-negotiable. Open to senior remote data engineering roles at ambitious companies. Not interested in relocation; I work remotely by choice and have built my life and routines around it.
interview_transcript:
Interviewer: Marco, thanks for joining. You're based in Milan — can you tell me more about your working arrangement?

Marco: Yes, I've been fully remote since 2020. My current role at Mediobanca is 100% remote — I work from my home office in Milan. That's a non-negotiable for me at this stage of my life. My partner works here, we have a house here. I wouldn't relocate to London, and I can't commit to being physically present in an office.

Interviewer: Our role requires five days a week at Canary Wharf in London. That would be a hard requirement.

Marco: I see. That's unfortunately not something I can do. I was perhaps not fully clear on that when I applied. I'm only looking at fully remote positions. I can do occasional travel to a client site — maybe a week here and there — but daily office attendance in another country isn't possible for me.

Interviewer: I understand. Would you need visa sponsorship to work in the UK if there were ever a remote arrangement?

Marco: Yes, as an EU national post-Brexit I would need a UK work visa. But as I said, remote is my only option. I hope I haven't wasted your time.

Interviewer: Not at all, we appreciate your honesty. The technical profile is excellent; it's just the location constraint. Let me note that for our records.

name: Eleanor Hawkins
cv:
ELEANOR HAWKINS
eleanor.hawkins@email.com | London, UK

PRINCIPAL DATA ENGINEER

PROFESSIONAL SUMMARY
Principal-level data engineer with 9 years of experience building enterprise data infrastructure across banking, insurance, and payments. Deep expertise in Spark, dbt, and data mesh architecture. Led teams of up to 8 engineers.

EXPERIENCE

Principal Data Engineer — HSBC, London (2020–present)
- Designed the bank's Group Data Platform architecture (data mesh) serving 40+ consuming teams
- Led a team of 8 engineers through a 2-year platform modernisation programme
- Introduced dbt at scale, defining standards adopted bank-wide
- Delivered SOX compliance data lineage project on time and under budget

Staff Data Engineer — Lloyds Banking Group, London (2016–2020)
- Senior engineering lead on credit data infrastructure and model feature stores

Data Engineer — Xchanging (now DXC), London (2015–2016)

SKILLS
Python, SQL, dbt, Spark, Snowflake, BigQuery, Airflow, Kafka, Terraform, Data Mesh architecture, team leadership

EDUCATION
BSc Mathematics — University of Nottingham (2012–2015)

RIGHT TO WORK
British citizen
linkedin: Principal data engineer and data platform architect with nine years in financial services. I've spent my career in environments where data accuracy and governance are existential concerns — banking and insurance. Currently leading a data mesh transformation at HSBC. Strong in Spark, dbt, and Snowflake. Based in London, happy to be in the office. Looking for principal or staff engineer roles where I can keep hands-on while having architecture influence.
interview_transcript:
Interviewer: Eleanor, you have an impressive background. Tell me about the data mesh work at HSBC.

Eleanor: It's been the most complex and rewarding thing I've worked on. Data mesh is easy to describe and very hard to implement in a large bank — there are so many legacy systems, organisational politics, and compliance constraints. We've been at it for two years and we're probably 60% of the way there. The shift to product thinking for data has been the biggest cultural challenge.

Interviewer: What's drawing you to this particular role? We're a much smaller company than HSBC.

Eleanor: That's exactly the draw. I've spent four years in big bank politics and I want to work somewhere where I can make decisions faster and see the impact more directly. Apex's payments focus is familiar territory.

Interviewer: The office setup is five days a week at Canary Wharf.

Eleanor: Fine by me. I'm in Elephant and Castle so that's straightforward.

Interviewer: Let's talk about compensation. We have a budget up to £85,000.

Eleanor: I appreciate the transparency. I'm currently on £105,000 at HSBC, and with my level of experience I'd need at least £105,000 to consider a move. I'm not in a position to take a significant pay cut even for a role I find interesting. Is there any flexibility on the budget?

Interviewer: Unfortunately our budget for this role is firm at £85,000 maximum. It sounds like there may be a gap here.

Eleanor: I think there might be, yes. I don't want to waste either of our time if the compensation can't be made to work.

linkedin:
Data engineer in London fintech — currently at Starling Bank building the data infrastructure that helps the operations and compliance teams sleep at night. Strong background in Python, dbt, and Snowflake. Five years of experience across digital banking and payments. Interested in fintech data engineering roles. UK-based on a Skilled Worker visa.

Note: My current visa is sponsored by Starling Bank. I am actively exploring my options given upcoming changes at my employer. Visa transfer to a new employer is straightforward for Skilled Worker roles, but I do require the new employer to act as a sponsor.
interview_transcript:
Interviewer: Raj, thanks for coming in today. Walk me through your experience at Starling.

Raj: I've been at Starling for four years now. I joined as a data engineer and I've grown into a senior role leading a pod of three engineers. My main area is financial data pipelines — the stuff that feeds reconciliation, CASS reporting, and the finance team's dashboards. It's a high-stakes environment because the numbers feed regulatory submissions.

Interviewer: Your profile looks excellent. I do need to ask about your visa situation — we saw a note on your LinkedIn. We do not offer visa sponsorship.

Raj: I understand. I'm currently on a Skilled Worker visa sponsored by Starling. To work for a new employer, I would need the new employer to be an approved UK Visas and Immigration sponsor and to sponsor the transfer. It's not a new visa application — it's a transfer — but it does require the employer to hold a sponsor licence.

Interviewer: I see. Unfortunately Apex doesn't currently hold a sponsor licence, and we have a policy of hiring candidates who have the right to work without sponsorship requirements. That would create a barrier here.

Raj: I appreciate you being clear about that. I was hoping it might not be an issue given it's a transfer rather than a new application, but I understand the position. Is there any possibility of the company obtaining a licence?

Interviewer: It's not something we're in a position to pursue at this stage. I'm sorry — your technical background is genuinely impressive.

interview_transcript:
Interviewer: Kieran, what draws you to financial data engineering after time in media and e-commerce?

Kieran: I'm genuinely attracted to the rigour of financial data. In media you can get away with approximate numbers; in fintech the reconciliation has to be exact. I find that challenge appealing. I've been picking up knowledge on the regulatory side in my own time — reading about CASS, MiFID data requirements.

Interviewer: Good to hear. What about the five-days-in-office arrangement?

Kieran: That's the one thing I'd want to talk through. I'm fully committed to this role and the company, but I've been hoping for a four-day working week. It's been a personal goal for a while — I coach youth football on Friday evenings and having a compressed schedule would let me manage that much better. Is there any flexibility there, or is five full days genuinely fixed?

Interviewer: It's fixed, unfortunately. The role requires five days at Canary Wharf — it's a team and culture thing, not just a policy.

Kieran: I understand. I'm not going to pretend it's ideal for me. I'd still be very interested in the role — I'm not going to walk away over it — but I want to be honest that it's a consideration for me.

Interviewer: We appreciate the honesty. Let's continue the conversation. Salary?

Kieran: I'm at £74,000. I'd be looking for £78–82,000.

Postdoctoral Researcher — University of Cambridge Computer Lab (2020–2022)
- Research on efficient computer vision architectures
- Co-supervised 2 PhD students

SKILLS
Python, PyTorch, JAX, MLflow, ONNX, NumPy, CUDA, LaTeX, Git

EDUCATION
PhD Machine Learning — University of Cambridge (2016–2020)
MEng Engineering — University of Cambridge (2012–2016)

RIGHT TO WORK
British citizen
linkedin: ML researcher working on efficient inference and neural architecture search at ARM Research. Cambridge-based. Four papers at NeurIPS/ICML. I care about fundamental research — understanding why things work, not just whether they work. My engineering skills are strong, but I'm most excited about roles that give me time and space for original research. Looking for research engineer or applied researcher roles ideally with academic collaboration or publication goals.
interview_transcript:
Interviewer: Nathaniel, your background is impressive. What draws you to an applied engineering role at Stratagem?

Nathaniel: Honestly, it's a bit of a shift from my current trajectory. I've been in research — ARM Research and before that a postdoc — and I'm thinking about what a more applied path looks like. The perception systems work at Stratagem is technically interesting and I'm curious about the defence domain.

Interviewer: The role is primarily engineering rather than research. There's some interesting problem-solving but we don't publish or run long-horizon research programmes. How do you feel about that?

Nathaniel: That's a genuine consideration for me. I won't pretend otherwise. I've built my identity around research and publication. I'd want to understand how much intellectual room there is in the role before committing. The engineering work would need to feel technically deep.

Interviewer: It is technically deep, but publication isn't part of it. The 2-days-onsite model — does that work?

Nathaniel: Cambridge works fine, yes. I live in Newnham, I could cycle in.

Interviewer: And security clearance? Do you hold any clearance?

Nathaniel: I don't currently hold clearance. I've worked in academia and ARM Research, which isn't classified. I'm aware that clearance would be needed and I'm UK national so presumably eligible, but I'd want to understand the timelines. DV takes a long time.

Interviewer: We can work with SC-eligible candidates and sponsor the DV if needed. Salary?

Nathaniel: I'm at £82,000 at ARM. I'd be looking for £85–95,000.

Interviewer: Isabelle, thanks for coming in. Tell me about your current role at Aviva.

Isabelle: I've been at Aviva for two years. It's been good for building fundamentals — I've led a dbt migration project and we're now on Snowflake. But it's a large, slow-moving organisation and I feel I've hit a ceiling in terms of technical growth. I want to work somewhere where the pace is faster and the technical bar is higher.

Interviewer: Tell me about your experience with event-driven architectures. We use Kafka heavily.

Isabelle: That's an area where I'm still developing. I've consumed from Kafka topics for one pipeline at Aviva, but I wouldn't call myself experienced with it. It's something I'm keen to learn.

Interviewer: And the office requirement — five days at Canary Wharf?

Isabelle: I'm generally fine with that. I do think there might be some days I'd prefer to work from home, but I understand if that's the policy. I'm in Hackney so it's manageable.

Interviewer: Salary expectations?

Isabelle: Well, this is a bit tricky to answer precisely. I'm on £60,000 now and I know I'd want to move upward. Something in the range of... I suppose somewhere around £70,000 to maybe £80,000? It would depend on the full package, the bonus structure, and other benefits. I'm not sure exactly what the right number is without knowing more.

Interviewer: I understand — our range for this role goes up to £85,000 for the right candidate. Do you have a sense of where in your range you'd need to land?

Isabelle: Honestly I'm not entirely sure. Somewhere comfortable in that zone, I think. I haven't done a lot of salary research for this specific level.

Oliver: That's an interesting question. At Luminance we sometimes work with government legal teams on sensitive documents, and I've signed some additional NDAs and data handling agreements. I think I'd be eligible for clearance — I'm UK national, no foreign ties — but I genuinely don't know if my past access counts for anything in the formal process.

interview_transcript:
Interviewer: Sienna, tell me about your experience managing a PM team.

Sienna: At Provenance I've had three direct reports for two years. I run weekly 1:1s, monthly team OKR reviews, and quarterly career development conversations. I've promoted one associate PM to PM in that time. I find people management one of the most rewarding parts of the role.

Interviewer: And the domain fit. We're a procurement workflow SaaS company — how does your sustainability background map to that?

Sienna: Supply chain and procurement are deeply linked to sustainability. I understand procurement workflows from the supplier-transparency angle — I've spent years thinking about how procurement teams make sourcing decisions. I think I'd bring a unique perspective on supplier relationships that most PM candidates wouldn't have.

Interviewer: We're a fully remote, async-first company. That works for you?

Sienna: Perfectly. I've been fully remote since 2019 and I advocate strongly for it.

Interviewer: I need to ask about something from your LinkedIn profile — you mention being committed to ethical business. Is that just values-level, or does it affect the companies you'd consider working for?

Sienna: That's an important question and I'll be honest with you. I do have a strong preference for working with B-corp certified companies or companies on a clear certification pathway. It matters to me at a deep level. CloudBridge isn't B-corp certified as far as I know. I'd want to understand the company's position on sustainability and whether certification is something you're working toward, before I'd feel fully committed to joining.

Interviewer: We're not currently pursuing B-corp certification. We take ethics seriously, but it's not a formal commitment.

Sienna: I see. I'd want to sit with that. It's not an automatic dealbreaker, but it's a significant consideration for me.

Interviewer: Kwame, thanks for coming in. Your content moderation background is quite different from our perception work — tell me how you see the connection.

Kwame: The core ML engineering skills are highly transferable — PyTorch, ONNX deployment, MLflow, model evaluation rigour. The domain is different but I've been studying computer vision on the side and I worked with some image-based classifiers at Jigsaw. I think the transition is manageable for someone with strong fundamentals.

Interviewer: The role involves defence applications. I want to ask directly — do you have any concerns about the nature of the work?

Kwame: I do want to think through that carefully. I care about the real-world impact of what I work on. Public safety applications I'm fully comfortable with. Lethal autonomous systems I would not want to be involved in. Can you tell me more about the specific end-use of the perception system?

Interviewer: Our systems are primarily used for border security, surveillance, and ISR — intelligence, surveillance, and reconnaissance. Not weapons targeting.

Kwame: That's helpful context. ISR and border security I can work with, as long as there are ethical oversight frameworks.

Interviewer: The 2-days-onsite arrangement works for you?

Kwame: Cambridge is doable. But I should mention something important to me that I want to be upfront about. I have a strong requirement to work for an employer that is committed to net-zero carbon operations and ideally has third-party verified environmental credentials. It's not negotiable for me — it's a core part of how I make career decisions. I'd want to see Stratagem's environmental commitments before accepting any offer.

Interviewer: We're a defence contractor — our carbon footprint is not something we've formally committed to measuring publicly. We do have internal sustainability policies.

Kwame: I see. That's something I'd need to investigate further. It's a significant factor for me.

Interviewer: Salary?

Kwame: I'm at £82,000. I'd need £88–95,000 given the domain learning and potential clearance process.

Chloe: I'm confident in the fundamentals and I learn fast. I'd expect to spend the first month or two contributing to existing models while I absorb the patterns, then gradually take on more ownership. I've read up on the staging/intermediate/mart pattern and I'm building practice projects.

Interviewer: Office — five days at Canary Wharf?

Chloe: Absolutely fine. I live in Peckham, so it's straightforward.

Interviewer: Salary?

Chloe: I'm at £52,000 currently. I'm looking for £60–65,000 given the domain change and technical step up.
